items.description: Vertical column of ~15 short entries in smaller script aligned down the outer right margin: p̂, h, po, ho, he, per, hau, par, hge, h., pe, po, ho, po, ki, pe; several with small connecting arcs or flourishes.
items.scholarly_note: Probationes pennae exercising common Old Norse particles and abbreviations (þó, þé, þer, þau, þar, þe etc.); may also function as informal glosses, line markers, or reader annotations. Ductus and ink consistent with use of the leaf in the 13th century.

items.description: Arabic numeral '25.' written in dark ink in the upper right corner, outside the text block.
items.scholarly_note: Post-medieval foliation or pagination mark, added during rebinding, cataloguing, or ownership (common for Codex Regius after its 17th-century royal presentation).

items.description: Large ornamental initial (Q- or O-form) with elongated descender descending several lines and curling leftward, marking the opening of a section.
items.scholarly_note: Decorated initial standard to the Codex Regius main hand to signal stanza or episode divisions in the Eddic poems (here within Þrymskviða); descender deliberately occupies left margin space.

items.description: Faint, partly rubbed or diluted ink traces along the bottom edge: letter-like forms (p-, u-, k-, a-, b-, v-), colons, dots, a possible 'd', and numeral-like '7' or '2'.
items.scholarly_note: Probationes pennae or fragmentary stray note; dilution and placement suggest either late addition, light erasure, or minor offset from the facing page.

items.description: Small cluster of abstract ink strokes in the bottom right below the text: a hook/curve, angled lines, a 'C'- or loop-like mark, and short stray strokes.
items.scholarly_note: Minor non-figurative pen trial or idle doodle; no clearly identifiable faces, animals, or zoomorphic forms.

items.description: Small curved arc or tie-line above one marginal 'po' entry plus an isolated curved stroke higher in the right margin column.
items.scholarly_note: Flourishes executed during the marginal pen-trial sequence.

items.description: Isolated letters showing thickened, retraced, or slightly overwritten ink strokes (notably near certain line endings and flagged repeated glyphs).
items.scholarly_note: Minor scribal self-corrections or second-pass inking by the main hand; typical of rapid copying on prepared vellum with iron-gall ink.

items.description: Small dark smudges and spots immediately adjacent to the marginal inscriptions.
items.scholarly_note: Ink smudges or handling stains associated with the pen activity that produced the marginal column.

scratches: Faint linear lighter streaks and creases across the lower third, running mostly horizontally with some diagonal components.
Surface abrasion marks near the bottom edge appearing as whitish lines against the aged vellum.
damage_notes: Widespread mottled brown staining and foxing, heaviest in upper left, top edge, outer right margin, and bottom; some spots encroach on the text block.
Irregular top right corner with small area of material loss or trimming.
General edge wear, slight cockling, and distortion of the vellum consistent with a heavily used 13th-century Icelandic leaf.
No major wormholes, large tears, or modern repairs visible; the leaf remains substantially intact.
Possible very faint text ghosting or offset ink from the facing page in the margins, largely masked by stronger staining.
summary: Page 34 (Þrymskviða) of Codex Regius presents a largely clean text block in the primary Gothic book hand, interrupted only by one prominent decorated initial with long descender. The standout non-text features are a structured vertical column of short marginal inscriptions in the right margin (pen trials of common ON particles/abbreviations) and a group of faint pen trials plus a small abstract doodle in the lower margin. A later '25.' foliation mark appears in the upper right. The parchment shows typical 13th-century Icelandic age staining, edge wear, minor surface abrasions, and isolated smudges, but lacks figurative doodles, clear holes, or extensive within-text corrections. The marginal activity indicates the leaf was also used for scribal practice beyond the main poetic copying.
